Each model family has its own tokenizer with a specific vocabulary and splitting rules. The tokenizer is not part of the neural network but is critical to model behavior.

A sampling strategy that restricts token selection to the k highest-probability tokens at each step, redistributing zero probability to all others. Top-k prevents very unlikely tokens from being sampled while preserving diversity. Common values are k=40 or k=50; lower k makes output more focused.
tldr: Top-k sampling restricts token selection to the k highest-probability tokens at each step, redistributing zero probability to all others.
deepDive: After computing the probability distribution, top-k sampling zeroes out all tokens except the k with the highest probability, then renormalizes the remaining k probabilities to sum to 1.0, and samples from this truncated distribution. The value of k is fixed regardless of the shape of the distribution.
whyItMatters: Top-k prevents very unlikely tokens from being sampled (reducing incoherence) while preserving diversity. Common values are k=40–50. The limitation of top-k is that the fixed k doesn't adapt to distribution shape: when the distribution is peaked, k=40 still allows many mediocre tokens; when it's flat, k=40 may exclude reasonable candidates.
examples: Vocabulary of 50,000 tokens, k=3: Only { "the": 0.50, "a": 0.30, "one": 0.20 } remain eligible. All other 49,997 tokens have zero probability of being selected.

definition: A sampling strategy that selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p, dynamically adjusting the candidate set based on distribution shape. At p=0.9, the model samples from the top tokens covering 90% of probability mass. Top-p is generally preferred over top-k because the effective vocabulary adapts to the distribution's entropy.
tldr: Top-p (nucleus) sampling selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p, dynamically adapting the candidate set to the shape of the distribution.
deepDive: Tokens are sorted by probability descending. Top-p adds tokens to the candidate set until their cumulative probability first exceeds the threshold p. The remaining tokens are zeroed out, and sampling is performed over the nucleus. Unlike top-k, the size of the nucleus varies: when the model is confident (peaked distribution), the nucleus may contain only 2–3 tokens; when uncertain (flat distribution), it may contain hundreds.
whyItMatters: Top-p adapts to distribution entropy, making it more robust than top-k across diverse prompts. At p=0.9, the model samples from tokens covering 90% of probability mass — excluding only the long tail of unlikely tokens. Top-p is generally the preferred sampling strategy and is often combined with temperature.
examples: p=0.9, sorted distribution: "the" = 0.55 (cumsum: 0.55) ← include, "a" = 0.25 (cumsum: 0.80) ← include, "one" = 0.12 (cumsum: 0.92 > 0.9) ← include (threshold crossed), all remaining tokens → excluded

definition: The dominant neural network architecture for LLMs, introduced by Vaswani et al. in "Attention Is All You Need" (2017). A Transformer consists of stacked blocks, each containing multi-head self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections and layer normalization. Transformers process entire sequences in parallel, unlike RNNs.
tldr: The dominant neural network architecture for LLMs, characterized by self-attention and parallel sequence processing.
deepDive: Introduced in 2017, the Transformer replaced sequential RNNs with a parallel architecture. It consists of stacked blocks of multi-head self-attention and feed-forward networks with residual connections. This design allows for scaling to trillions of tokens of data.
whyItMatters: The Transformer is the 'engine' of the AI revolution. Every major breakthrough in the last 5 years (GPT, Claude, Gemini) is built on this specific architectural foundation.
examples: All modern LLMs are Transformers.

definition: The elapsed time from when an API request is sent to when the first output token is received. TTFT is dominated by the prefill computation (processing the prompt). Minimizing TTFT is critical for low-latency interactive applications. Prompt caching can reduce TTFT significantly for long repeated prefixes.
tldr: Time to First Token (TTFT) is the elapsed time from when an API request is sent to when the first output token is received — the primary latency metric for interactive LLM applications.
deepDive: TTFT is dominated by the prefill phase: processing the entire input prompt through the transformer to populate the KV cache. Prefill computation scales with prompt length — longer prompts mean higher TTFT. After prefill, the first token is generated and streamed immediately, starting the decode phase.
whyItMatters: TTFT determines how quickly the UI can start rendering a response. For chat applications, TTFT above 500ms starts to feel sluggish. Voice applications require TTFT under 200ms. Prompt caching is the most effective way to reduce TTFT for applications with long repeated prompt prefixes.
examples: Short prompt (100 tokens): TTFT ≈ 80ms, Medium prompt (2,000 tokens): TTFT ≈ 400ms, Long prompt (20,000 tokens): TTFT ≈ 2,500ms, With prompt caching (cached 20K prefix): TTFT ≈ 80ms (effectively short prompt)

definition: A subword tokenization algorithm that starts with a large initial vocabulary and iteratively removes tokens that least increase the total log-likelihood of the training corpus under a unigram language model assumption. Unigram produces a probabilistic vocabulary and is used by SentencePiece (T5, XLNet, mBART).

A database optimized for storing, indexing, and querying high-dimensional embedding vectors at scale. Vector databases use approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) algorithms (HNSW, IVF) for fast similarity search. Examples include Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Chroma, and pgvector. They are the storage backbone of RAG pipelines.

The finite set of tokens a model can produce, typically ranging from 32K to 256K tokens. Each token corresponds to a unique integer ID. The vocabulary is determined by the tokenizer at training time and is fixed at inference. Most modern LLMs use vocabularies of 100K–130K tokens.

An individual learnable parameter in a neural network — a floating-point number stored in a weight matrix. LLMs contain billions to trillions of weights organized into embedding matrices, attention projection matrices, and feed-forward layer matrices. Weights encode all the model's "knowledge" and are updated during training.
tldr: An individual learnable parameter in a neural network — a numerical value that determines the strength of a connection between neurons.
deepDive: LLMs contain billions to trillions of weights organized into matrices. These weights are updated during training via backpropagation to minimize the prediction error. Weights effectively encode the 'knowledge' the model has acquired from its training data.
whyItMatters: Weights are what you are downloading when you download a model from Hugging Face. The precision of these weights (e.g., FP16 vs INT4) determines the model's size and performance (Quantization).
examples: A model with 7 billion weights is a '7B' model.

definition: A subword tokenization algorithm used by BERT and related models. Similar to BPE, WordPiece merges token pairs — but instead of frequency, it selects merges that maximize the likelihood of training data. WordPiece vocabularies are typically 30K tokens; subword tokens that continue a word are prefixed with ##.

Prompting the model to perform a task with no demonstrations — relying entirely on instructions and the model's pre-trained knowledge. Zero-shot capability is a hallmark of large-scale pre-training and instruction tuning; it allows LLMs to tackle novel tasks immediately without curating examples.
tldr: Zero-shot learning is prompting the model to perform a task with no examples — relying entirely on instructions and the model's pre-trained knowledge.
deepDive: The prompt contains only the task description and the input to process — no demonstrations. The model must infer the desired behavior purely from the instruction and patterns internalized during pre-training and instruction tuning. Zero-shot capability emerges from training on diverse instruction-following data at scale.
whyItMatters: Zero-shot capability is a hallmark of modern LLMs and the baseline for any new task. It allows LLMs to tackle novel tasks immediately without curating examples, which is valuable when labeled examples are unavailable or the task changes frequently. When zero-shot fails, adding one-shot or few-shot examples is the first remedy.
examples: Zero-shot prompt: "Classify the sentiment of the following review as Positive, Negative, or Neutral: 'The battery life is decent but the screen is disappointing.'". Model output: "Negative" (No examples provided — the model applies its general understanding of sentiment analysis.)

definition: The tokens generated by the model in its response to a prompt. Also called output tokens or sampled tokens. Completion tokens are produced one at a time autoregressively and are typically priced higher than prompt tokens in API billing because each token requires a sequential forward pass.

An attention variant where query heads are grouped and each group shares a single key and value head. GQA reduces KV cache memory without the quality loss of fully sharing K/V across all heads (Multi-Query Attention). Used by LLaMA 3, Mistral, and Gemma to balance speed and quality.
tldr: An attention variant where query heads are grouped and each group shares a single key and value head to reduce memory usage.
deepDive: GQA sits between Multi-Head Attention (MHA) and Multi-Query Attention (MQA). It reduces KV cache memory footprint without the significant quality loss seen in MQA. This is crucial for maintaining performance as context windows grow.
whyItMatters: GQA significantly lowers the VRAM requirements for long-context inference. It allows models like Llama 3 to process 128K tokens on consumer-grade hardware that would otherwise crash under standard MHA.
examples: Llama 3 and Mistral 7B use GQA to balance speed, memory, and reasoning quality.

definition: Techniques for extending an LLM's effective context window beyond what it was trained on. Methods include RoPE scaling (YaRN, LongRoPE), continued pre-training on longer sequences, and selective attention mechanisms. Extended context enables long-document understanding, entire codebase analysis, and multi-hour conversation memory.
tldr: Techniques for extending an LLM's effective context window beyond the specific length it was initially pre-trained on.
deepDive: Methods include RoPE scaling (YaRN, LongRoPE), continued pre-training on longer sequences, and selective attention mechanisms. These techniques manipulate the positional embeddings to allow the model to maintain focus and coherence over much longer spans than its original training limit.
whyItMatters: Extended context enables game-changing use cases like long-document understanding, entire codebase analysis, and multi-hour conversation memory. It reduces the need for aggressive RAG chunking.
examples: Using RoPE scaling to take a model trained on 4,000 tokens and allowing it to process 128,000 tokens with minimal accuracy loss.
